06/16/1990    15:26    **** PANAFRX  UF-400 ****    4155821055    06605446    P.02

                                    LAW OFFICES OF

                            BAGATELOS & FADEM
                                THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
BARRY FADEM                         601 CALIFORNIA STREET                    TELEPHONE
PETER A. BAGATELOS                  SUITE 1801                              (415) 982-7100
                            SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
                                                                             FAX
                                                                             (415) 982-1085

                                    August 16, 1990


MEMORANDUM:

To:     Deanne Stone, Esq.

From:   Wes Van Winkle
        Of Counsel

Re:     Impact Of Proposition 105 Disclaimer Requirements On
        Campaign Buttons, Pins, Bumperstickers, And Other Items


        As you know, this firm serves as legal counsel to Taxpayers
for Common Sense, the committee which opposed the passage of
Proposition 134.  On behalf of this committee, I am writing to
request guidance from the legal division with regard to the
impact of Proposition 105 "major funding" disclaimer
requirements on such traditional campaign items as, e.g.
campaign buttons, bottleneck hanger cards and other items on
which disclaimers would be impractical.

        Taxpayers for Common Sense intends to produce automobile
bumperstickers, campaign buttons, bottleneck hanger cards,
restaurant table "tent" cards, back-bar electronic digital
read-out messages and other similar items for distribution at
the "point-of-sale" of petri wine and distilled spirits.  On
some of these items, such as campaign buttons, the "major
funding" disclaimer required by Proposition 105 simply cannot be
printed in a size sufficient to be legible, unless the actual
message on the campaign button is totally obscured.  On other
items, such as bumperstickers, it might be possible to print a
disclaimer, but the nature of the item itself would render the
disclaimer an absurdity.  In the case of a back-bar electronic
digital read-out sign, the message which the campaign wishes to
convey is simply "134 No." The required disclaimer would be
many times longer than the message, and would make the
expenditure for the display many times more expensive.

        In addition, the campaign has discussed the possibility of
using other unique and novel methods of communication.  For
example, the campaign may wish to flash our electronic message
at football audiences from the side of the Goodyear Blimp, or
hire a skywriter to write "No on 134" in the air over
Candlestick Park during a 49er game.  Will the FPPC require

08/15/1990    15:26    **** PANAFAX UF-400 ****    4153821085    06605446    P.03

Memorandum
August 16, 1990
Page Two

major funding disclaimers under these circumstances?

As we have discussed, Title 11 of the Federal Code of
Regulations specifically exempts certain communication methods
from the disclaimer requirements applicable in federal
campaigns.  For example, Section 110.11(a)(2) states that the
Federal requirements "do not apply to bumerstickers, pins,
pens, buttons, and similar small items on which the disclaimer
can not be conveniently printed."  The requirements also "do not
apply to skywriting, watchtowers or other means of displaying an
advertisement of such a nature that the inclusion of the
disclaimer would be impracticable." (11C.F.R.§110.11(a)(2))

     It is my belief that Mr. Rogers' intent in drafting
Proposition 105 was to require "major funding" disclaimers
primarily on radio and television advertisements—the primary
method by which ballot measure campaigns communicate with
California voters.  I do not believe that he ever considered
such problems as campaign buttons, skywriters or other means of
communications not normally thought of as "advertising."  I
therefore recommend that the staff consider limiting the
coverage of Proposition 105 to radio, television, newspaper, and
magazine advertisements, which were clearly in Mr. Rogers'
contemplation, and the contemplation of the voters, when
Proposition 105 was enacted.  To extend the disclaimer
requirements beyond this point renders the disclaimers an
absurdity.

     Thank you for expediting this advice request.  The campaign
season is now upon us, and my client is under considerable time
pressure to plan, print, and distribute the items I have
discussed above.  Please do not hesitate to contact me if there
is any additional information I can provide.


WVW/jjm

TCS:8-16ds
